# Transcrição: 03-Third Person

- **Fonte:** 03-Third Person
- **Duração:** 02:29
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:29]

It's not going to take long to talk about third person storytelling. Limit it only to objects and things. If you're telling a story about a person, you should never be using the third person. It's the least emotionally connective version of storytelling. It's the most dangerous version of storytelling because it doesn't allow you to focus the audience's attention on anything specifically. It's a terrible way to tell stories about people. It is unfortunately sometimes the only way we can tell stories about objects. If you have to tell a story about the lawnmower that your company is selling and you cannot infuse yourself into that story, you probably have to talk about that lawnmower from the third person. To speak about it from the second person, I want you to see a lawnmower. I want you to see the wheels. I want you to see the blade. It might work, but you can probably feel it's a little weird. It's a little odd. It's a little manipulative. It doesn't work very often. It can work if the object that you're dealing with has some emotional resonance to it. If you're selling stuffed animals, you can use the second person. I want you to see the teddy bear. I want you to see the little girl who will one day be holding the teddy bear. It works with lawnmowers, not so much. Software, not really. Computer chips, tough to make an emotional second person appeal to a computer chip. So we save the third person, the ugly stepchild of storytelling, for only those moments when the other two will not work for us. We save them for objects that are lacking any kind of emotional appeal at all. We save it for moments when the objects we're speaking about are not conducive to storytelling. We avoid it whenever possible. It's necessary, but it's like broccoli. It's not great. It will work for you. It will probably do the job, but it's not ideal. So save your third person storytelling for only those moments when it's absolutely needed, because it is by far the least effective version of storytelling.
